Vileg Yedahalli Mysore Taluku Yelawala Hobali
Raveendra To I Just Don'T Want To Be

Vikas S.strength: Sharpest discovery and feasibility framing of the group: he converts land parcel size into buildable area on the fly, applies the Hilton radius rule, and sets honest expectations rather than over-promising, which lands well with informed owners.
Vikas S.risk: Several calls dissolve into heavy code-switching and disjointed exchanges where the thread is lost and little is established, and he occasionally under-sells by leading with caveats ("I'm not assuring you anything") before any value is built.
Vikas S.insight: Vikas leans on a credibility-through-honesty posture ("we don't give fake promises, only commit if we can make business for at least a year") and shows real Spark-by-Hilton operating literacy — the 3.5km radius guardrail, FAR-to-built-area math, an already-signed Mysore property — while also being the only BD who fluently works the Open Hotels line as a separate, no-lock-in budget option.

Sukhpreet Singh Lohia.strength: Strong, confident rapport and a clear command of the Embassy-Hilton positioning, including the accurate premium-economy framing of Spark against IBIS, Ginger and Bloom.
Sukhpreet Singh Lohia.risk: Calls drift into long monologues and repetition, and he advances prospects who lack a property or readiness (lease-only, "not interested now") instead of qualifying out, plus an opening name fumble undercuts the polished intro.
Sukhpreet Singh Lohia.insight: Sukhpreet leans hard on Embassy's developer credentials (50M sqft, tech park, WeWork, Hilton license) as his opener, which builds authority but often arrives before he has qualified whether the prospect even controls a site.

Prasoon Singh.strength: He qualifies room count early and disqualifies cleanly when a property is too small (correctly passing on the 27-room Amritsar lead) rather than forcing the conversation.
Prasoon Singh.risk: The opener stays generic "Olive Hospitality" with no Spark-vs-Olive story, and frequent garbled, off-topic utterances make several calls incoherent, so prospects disengage before any value is conveyed.
Prasoon Singh.insight: His new calls are reflexively opened around an unverified "you showed interest in our marketing campaign" hook, which several prospects clearly do not recognize, so most calls collapse before any brand framing happens.

Preetham D Almeida.strength: Methodical discovery and clear product-fit logic: he pulls land size and location first, then matches Olive vs Spark to the parcel and explains the operator-on-behalf-of-Hilton model plainly.
Preetham D Almeida.risk: His room-count guidance is inconsistent with the brand book ("above 50 rooms, 50-150" and a 30-key Olive start), and several calls degrade into heavily garbled, repetitive stretches that blur the pitch.
Preetham D Almeida.insight: Preetham runs a dual-product play, sizing land parcels and slotting prospects into either Olive (smaller footprints, management contract) or Spark by Hilton, then promising a feasibility study as the bridge to a meeting.

Krishna Kumar (Nakkani).strength: Disciplined qualification: he probes floors/room counts up front, correctly states Spark needs roughly 60 keys, and redirects sub-threshold or approval-light sites to Olive rather than forcing a misfit.
Krishna Kumar (Nakkani).risk: He sometimes mislabels the brand he represents ("All Your Hotels by MSA Group") and, when a prospect openly wants capital, keeps the door ajar ("I'll check if there's a possibility") instead of clearly resetting expectations.
Krishna Kumar (Nakkani).insight: In the 24h window Krishna consistently checks room-count feasibility early and pivots cleanly to Olive by Embassy when a site is too small for Spark, showing genuine cross-brand command.

Tabrez Anwar.strength: Clear, structured explanation of the operating model — he articulates the master service agreement scope (operations, marketing, bookings, OTA, security) crisply and qualifies room counts, citing the "we don't do Olive under 30" threshold plainly.
Tabrez Anwar.risk: He describes the brand as "3 Star" (off-positioning) and several calls show shaky lead verification and brand mix-ups (Spark vs Olive, region ownership), which can erode trust early when the owner is probing who actually runs the show.
Tabrez Anwar.insight: Tabrez consistently anchors on the Embassy-Hilton master service agreement and Hilton's global scale/loyalty reach as his credibility hook, and adapts into Bengali for East-India owners, but he twice mislabels the brand tier as "3 Star" and lets a few calls run on identity confusion.

Ankur Pateriya.strength: He sizes each lead quickly — location, land area, rooms, main-road frontage — and channels prospects to the right brand, correctly steering an agricultural-land owner and a too-small 20-room owner away from Spark.
Ankur Pateriya.risk: His delivery is heavily garbled and rushed across calls, the Embassy/Olive/Spark framing often comes out as fragmented half-sentences, and he leans on a deferred "team will check feasibility, send location on WhatsApp" close rather than conveying the brand story in the call.
Ankur Pateriya.insight: In the last 24h Ankur ran a high-volume MP outbound sweep (Bhopal/Indore/Ujjain) where he consistently gates on the brand minimums up front, citing the Spark/Hilton 50-room floor and routing smaller or lease-only leads toward Olive instead.

Iqura Hussain.strength: Not observable from the available transcript content; the one call's body is not present to assess.
Iqura Hussain.risk: Insufficient data this window — a single short call with no captured dialogue means coverage and pitch quality cannot be evaluated.
Iqura Hussain.insight: With only a single short call in the window and no transcript body captured, the read is limited and no reliable pattern can be drawn this window.

Shiva Srivastava.strength: He is consistent on basic qualification — asking land size, room count, land usage and operational status early — and correctly steers smaller or sub-threshold properties toward Olive by Embassy and flags the 50-plus-room bar for Hilton.
Shiva Srivastava.risk: When a prospect pushes back hard (the Dalhousie caller who challenged trust, prior deals and BD coverage), Shiva loses control of the call and gets flustered rather than steadying it, and many of his shorter calls dissolve into repeated filler and dead air that stall the conversation before any real discovery.
Shiva Srivastava.insight: In calls since yesterday, Shiva opens almost every call by identifying only as "Hilton Hotel" and stating a 50-plus-room requirement, but rarely builds the Spark "elevated essentials" premium-economy story, so the brand lands as a generic name rather than a distinct proposition.

Mohd Zaib.strength: He runs a genuine discovery sequence — rooms, land size, window/sunlight, lift, floor count, location context — before pitching, and his stronger calls carry a confident expansion-and-occupancy narrative that frames the brand credibly rather than just naming it.
Mohd Zaib.risk: He repeatedly cites a 50-room minimum for Spark when the brand is 60-150, and in at least one call let a prospect's "4-star rating" assumption stand uncorrected, so the premium-economy positioning is being undermined by his own inaccurate framing; several calls also trail off into vague "I'll share details" endings without a firm anchor.
Mohd Zaib.insight: In calls since yesterday, Zaib reliably reaches for the right brand by property size — opening the fuller Spark "premium economy" story (4,000-8,000 room rent, occupancy comparisons vs Garden Inn/DoubleTree, expansion footprint) on larger leads and cleanly pivoting to Olive by Embassy when room counts fall below the Spark threshold.

Nitish Choudhary.strength: He keeps the two brands clearly separated with the correct room thresholds (Olive 30-35, Spark minimum 60) and cleanly refuses JV-only owners without souring the call.
Nitish Choudhary.risk: In the longer existing-hotel call his side of the dialogue turns fragmented and hard to follow, so the qualification thread can get lost mid-conversation.
Nitish Choudhary.insight: Nitish leads with crisp eligibility gating — the room minimums and the management-only/no-JV rule — which qualifies or disqualifies prospects fast without over-pitching before he has floor plans and location.

Gourang Patil.strength: Disciplined, high-frequency outreach with strong rapport and steady discovery — he reliably qualifies location, room count and build status, and cleanly disqualifies poor fits (the Airbnb partner, the small hostel) instead of forcing them.
Gourang Patil.risk: Spark by Hilton is repeatedly presented without its elevated-essentials/premium-economy identity and saddled with the generic 30-room minimum, and in one call he wandered into category confusion ("category is 8", "business hotel"); the Olive room-size/minimum framing is applied loosely across both brands.
Gourang Patil.insight: Gourang reuses a tight, repeatable script (Embassy REIT proof via WeWork/Hard Rock/India Bulls, then the AI manpower-ratio and 85% occupancy story) and switches fluidly between Hindi, Marathi and English to match each owner, but the Spark narrative is consistently reduced to a name-drop rather than its own positioning.

Amrit Mishra.strength: He articulates the Spark positioning accurately as premium-economy/upper-mid-scale, openly contrasts it with 5-star, and is honest when a remote leisure parcel is a difficult fit rather than overselling it.
Amrit Mishra.risk: He repeatedly states the Spark room minimum as 50 (and once "around 50-60"), which understates the 60-key floor and could mis-set developer expectations.
Amrit Mishra.insight: Amrit runs each call like a feasibility consultation, using live map and FSI math to size buildable rooms, which lets him reframe an under-sized parcel into an 80-90 room opportunity and keep the prospect engaged.
